title: Guard Rotation at the Gate
desc: Stand watch at the hive entrance. Check credentials. Stay awake.

steps.text:
Queen's Gate is a massive stone archway carved with hive symbols, wide enough for ten Danians abreast. Every creature entering or exiting passes through here, and every one of them needs credentials.

The gate captain reviews your shift assignment. "Eight hours. Check every ID glyph — forged glyphs are getting better. Last week someone tried to enter with a glyph that was three seasons old and upside down. We caught it. Barely."

She shows you the verification technique — hold the glyph to the recognition stone, watch for the color response. Blue is valid. Red is invalid. Yellow means the glyph is real but expired.

"What about green?"

"Green means run. Green means the glyph belongs to someone who shouldn't be here at all. I've seen green once in twelve years. Let's keep it that way."

options.label: Take the main gate position — high traffic
Take the auxiliary gate — lower traffic, better view

steps.text:
Main gate traffic is relentless. Workers heading to the fungus gardens. Mandiblors rotating shifts. Traders with carts of supplies. Each one hands you their glyph, you press it to the stone, you check the color. Blue. Blue. Blue. Yellow — you flag it for renewal. Blue. Blue.

Three hours in, you're in a rhythm. The glyphs become automatic — hand, stone, check, return. The creatures blur together. Which is dangerous, the gate captain warned. Complacency is the forger's best friend.

Hour five: a glyph doesn't feel right. The weight is off — slightly too light. You press it to the stone. Blue. Valid. But the weight nags at you. You look at the creature — standard Danian worker, nothing unusual. You check the glyph again. The carving is perfect. The color response is correct.

But the edge. The glyph's edge is smooth. Real glyphs have a micro-texture from the minting process — a roughness you can feel with your thumb. This edge is polished smooth. Someone made a perfect visual copy but couldn't replicate the mint texture.

You flag it. The gate captain examines the glyph under magnification. Her expression tightens.

"Forged. A good one." She signals the Mandiblor detail. The creature is detained without incident — confused, frightened, claiming they bought the glyph from a vendor. Maybe true. Maybe not.

"Good catch," the captain says. "Most guards wouldn't have felt the edge difference. You might have a future at the gate."

options.label: Finish the shift with sharpened attention

steps.text:
The auxiliary gate handles overflow traffic and special deliveries — larger carts, delegations, anything that doesn't fit through the main gate's standard lane. It's slower, which gives you time to actually look at who's entering.

From the auxiliary position, you can also see Oipont's Lookout above — the elevated post where scouts watch the approaches for incoming threats. The lookout signals are your early warning: one flag for routine traffic, two for unexpected visitors, three for potential hostiles.

Most of the day is one-flag routine. Supply carts, mostly. You check credentials, inspect cargo (nothing prohibited — no foreign biologicals, no unregistered minerals, no live creatures without containment permits), and wave them through.

Late in the shift, the lookout flies two flags. Unexpected visitors. You see them approaching — three creatures, not Danian, moving at a diplomatic pace. An OverWorld delegation, based on the formal cloaks.

You check their papers. Valid embassy credentials, properly sealed. Their cargo is diplomatic pouches — exempt from inspection by treaty. You log them, assign an escort, and watch them enter.

The gate captain reviews your log. "OverWorld delegation. Third one this month." She makes a note. "They're negotiating something. Nobody tells the gate guards what." She shrugs. "Our job is the gate, not the politics behind it."

options.label: Close the shift log and hand off
